records.ety: [OE. cranke; akin to E. cringe, cringle, crinkle, and to crank, a., the root meaning, probably, “to turn, twist.” See Cringe.]

defs: A bent portion of an axle, or shaft, or an arm keyed at right angles to the end of a shaft, by which motion is imparted to or received from it; also used to change circular into reciprocating motion, or reciprocating into circular motion. See Bell crank.
Any bend, turn, or winding, as of a passage.
quotes: So many turning cranks these have, so many crooks.

defs: A twist or turn in speech; a conceit consisting in a change of the form or meaning of a word.
quotes: Quips, and cranks, and wanton wiles.

defs: A twist or turn of the mind; caprice; whim; crotchet; also, a fit of temper or passion.
quotes: Violent of temper; subject to sudden cranks.

defs: A person full of crotchets; one given to fantastic or impracticable projects; one whose judgment is perverted in respect to a particular matter.

quotes: Thou art a counterfeit crank, a cheater.

records.ety: [AS. cranc weak; akin to Icel. krangr, D. & G. krank sick, weak (cf. D. krengen to careen). Cf. Crank, n.]

defs: Liable to careen or be overset, as a ship when she is too narrow, or has not sufficient ballast, or is loaded too high, to carry full sail.
Full of spirit; brisk; lively; sprightly; overconfident; opinionated.
quotes: He who was, a little before, bedrid, . . . was now crank and lusty.
If you strong electioners did not think you were among the elect, you would not be so crank about it.

defs: To run with a winding course; to double; to crook; to wind and turn.
quotes: See how this river comes me cranking in.
